tags: Python for Everyone a blog series
content:
Welcome to the Series

Hi there! Welcome to Python for Everyone a blog series where we learn Python from
scratch, step by step, all the way up to Artificial Intelligence and Machine Learning. Whether you have never written a single line of code before, or you have tried Python once and felt confused, this series is for you.
Goal: By the end of this blog series, you will understand Python well enough to read, write, and work with real AI and ML code. 

Each post in this series builds on the previous one. So do not skip ahead start here and enjoy the journey!

What is Python?

Python is a programming language. A programming language is just a way to talk to a computer and tell it what to do. Python is one of the most popular languages in the world right now  and for good reasons.

Why Python?

  • Easy to read: Python code looks almost like plain English.

  • Beginner-friendly: You do not need to set up complicated things just to start.

  • Powerful: Big companies like Google, Netflix, Instagram, and NASA use Python.

  • Great for AI/ML: Almost all AI and Machine Learning tools are built with Python.

  • Huge community: Millions of people use Python, so it is easy to find help online.

Who created python ?

Python was created by Guido van Rossum, a Dutch programmer. He started working on it in the late 1980s and released the first version in 1991. His goal was simple: make a language that is easy for humans to read and write. He succeeded!

Fun Fact: Python is NOT named after the snake. Guido named it after the British comedy show 'Monty Python's Flying Circus'  he wanted the language to be fun!

Python 2 vs Python 3 Which One?

You might hear people talk about Python 2 and Python 3. Here is what you need to know:

  • Python 2: Old. Released in 2000. It is no longer supported since 2020. Do NOT use it.

  • Python 3: New, modern, and actively developed. Always use Python 3.

All the code in this blog series will use Python 3. When you install Python, always pick the latest Python 3 version.

How to Install Python

Before we write any code, we need Python installed on our computer. Follow these steps:

Step 1. Download Python

  • Go to the official website: python.org

  • Click the big 'Download Python 3.x.x' button.

  • The website will automatically show the right version for your computer (Windows, Mac, or Linux).

Step 2. Install Python

  • Windows: Run the downloaded file. IMPORTANT: Check the box that says "Add Python to PATH" before clicking Install.

  • Mac: Run the downloaded package and follow the steps.

  • Linux: Python 3 is usually already installed. You can check by typing python3 --version in the terminal.

Step 3. Check the Installation

Open your terminal (Command Prompt on Windows, Terminal on Mac/Linux) and type:

python3 --version

You should see something like:

Python 3.12.0

If you see a version number, Python is installed correctly. If not, re-do Step 1 and 2.

Choosing a Code Editor

A code editor is like a special notebook for writing code. You could use Notepad, but a proper editor makes your life much easier. We recommend these:

VS Code (Best Choice for Beginners)

  • Free and made by Microsoft.

  • Very popular and has great Python support.

  • Download at: code.visualstudio.com

Other Options

  • PyCharm: Very powerful, but heavier. Has a free Community edition.

  • Thonny: Super simple, designed specifically for beginners.

  • Jupyter Notebook: Great for data science and AI/ML. We will use this later in the series.

For now, just install VS Code and the official Python extension from the VS Code marketplace.

Your Very First Python Program

The tradition in programming is that your first program prints the words "Hello, World!" on the screen. Let us do that.

Open VS Code and create a new file

  • Click File → New File

  • Save it as hello.py (the .py tells your computer it is a Python file)

Write the code

# My very first Python program
print("Hello, World!")

Run the code

  • Open the Terminal in VS Code (View → Terminal)

  • Type: python3 hello.py and press Enter

Output

Hello, World!

Congratulations! You just wrote and ran your first Python program!

Understanding What We Just Wrote

Let us look at the code again line by line:

# My very first Python program
print("Hello, World!")

Line 1. The Comment

The # symbol starts a comment. A comment is a note for humans — Python completely ignores it. Use comments to explain what your code does. Good programmers write lots of comments!

Line 2. The print() Function

The print() function tells Python to show something on the screen. Whatever you put inside the brackets () gets displayed. The text must be inside quotes "Hello, World!".

In Python, a piece of text is called a 'string'. Strings always go inside quotes. You can use single quotes ' ' or double quotes " ".

Key Concepts to Know Before Next Post

1. Statements

Each line of Python code is called a statement. Python reads and runs your statements one by one, from top to bottom.

print("First")
print("Second")
print("Third")

Output:

First
Second
Third

2. Case Sensitivity

Python is case-sensitive. This means print, Print, and PRINT are all different things. Only print (lowercase) is the real function.

print("This works")
Print("This gives an error") # Wrong! Capital P

3. Errors are Normal

Every programmer in the world gets errors. An error is just Python telling you something needs to be fixed. Do not be afraid of errors — read the message carefully and try to understand what Python is saying.

When you get an error, read the last line of the error message first. It usually tells you exactly what went wrong.

What's Coming in Blog Post 2? 

Now that you have Python installed and ran your first program, we are ready to go deeper. In the next post, we will learn about:

  • Variables: How to store information in your program

  • Data Types: Numbers, text, True/False values

  • Basic Operations: Math and working with text

  • User Input: How to ask the user to type something

Make sure you practice the code from this post before moving on. Try changing 'Hello, World!' to your own name!

The Full Series Roadmap

Here is the big picture of where we are headed. We will go from the very basics all the way to AI and Machine Learning:


Phase		topicgoal
Basics

Setup, Variables, Data Types, Loops, Functions

Write simple Python programs

Intermediate

OOP, Files, Error Handling, Modules, Libraries

 Build real projects

Data Science

NumPy, Pandas, Matplotlib, Data Cleaning

Work with data and charts

Machine Learning

Scikit-Learn, Regression, Classification, Clustering

Build ML models

Deep Learning

TensorFlow / PyTorch, Neural Networks, CNNs, RNNs

 Build AI systems

AI Projects

NLP, Computer Vision, Transformers, LLMs

  Real-world AI apps

It looks like a lot, but remember, we go one small step at a time. Every post will build on the last. By the time we reach AI/ML, all the basics will feel natural.

See You in Post 2!
